agents.prompt:
You are a web discovery agent for a research pipeline. Your task is to find and collect sources comparing RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) vs long-context LLMs for document question answering.

Research Question: "How do RAG and long-context LLMs compare for document question answering tasks?"

## Required searches (execute ALL):

### Primary queries:
1. "RAG vs long context LLM document question answering benchmark comparison 2024 2025"
2. "retrieval augmented generation versus long context window performance document QA"
3. "Loogle benchmark RAG long context results"
4. "InfBench infinite bench long context vs RAG"

### Negative queries (mandatory):
5. "RAG fails document question answering limitations"
6. "long context LLMs hallucinate retrieval needle in haystack"
7. "when not to use RAG retrieval augmented generation"
8. "limitations of long context window LLMs attention"

## For each search:
- Use web_search to find relevant sources
- For the top 3-5 most relevant results, use fetch_url to read the content
- Extract: title, URL, source_type (paper/article/blog/benchmark), year, a 1-2 sentence summary, and relevance score (1-5)

## Output format:
After all searches, compile a structured source inventory as a markdown table:

```markdown
| ID | Title | URL | Type | Year | Relevance | Summary |
|----|-------|-----|------|------|-----------|---------|
| W1 | ... | ... | paper | 2024 | 5 | ... |
```

Also include a "negative_search" section listing what contrary evidence was found.

Target: 5-10 high-quality sources. Prioritize papers, benchmarks, and technical articles over marketing content.

assignment.objective:
You are a web discovery agent for a research pipeline. Your task is to find and collect sources comparing RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) vs long-context LLMs for document question answering.

Research Question: "How do RAG and long-context LLMs compare for document question answering tasks?"

## Required searches (execute ALL):

### Primary queries:
1. "RAG vs long context LLM document question answering benchmark comparison 2024 2025"
2. "retrieval augmented generation versus long context window performance document QA"
3. "Loogle benchmark RAG long context results"
4. "InfBench infinite bench long context vs RAG"

### Negative queries (mandatory):
5. "RAG fails document question answering limitations"
6. "long context LLMs hallucinate retrieval needle in haystack"
7. "when not to use RAG retrieval augmented generation"
8. "limitations of long context window LLMs attention"

## For each search:
- Use web_search to find relevant sources
- For the top 3-5 most relevant results, use fetch_url to read the content
- Extract: title, URL, source_type (paper/article/blog/benchmark), year, a 1-2 sentence summary, and relevance score (1-5)

## Output format:
After all searches, compile a structured source inventory as a markdown table:

```markdown
| ID | Title | URL | Type | Year | Relevance | Summary |
|----|-------|-----|------|------|-----------|---------|
| W1 | ... | ... | paper | 2024 | 5 | ... |
```

Also include a "negative_search" section listing what contrary evidence was found.

Target: 5-10 high-quality sources. Prioritize papers, benchmarks, and technical articles over marketing content.

agents.result:
I have collected substantial evidence. Let me compile the final structured report now.

### SUMMARY
I executed all 8 required searches (4 primary, 4 negative) and fetched content from 14+ sources. The search landscape reveals rich recent activity (2024-2025) directly comparing RAG and long-context LLMs for document QA. Key finding: the consensus from multiple peer-reviewed papers (EMNLP 2024, ACL 2024, ICML 2025) is that **long-context LLMs generally outperform RAG in QA accuracy when sufficiently resourced, but RAG retains significant cost and efficiency advantages**. The LaRA benchmark (ICML 2025) and the Li et al. (2025) revisits paper both show that optimal choice depends on model size, context length, task type, and retrieved chunk characteristics — there is no universal silver bullet. The negative searches confirmed well-documented limitations on both sides: RAG suffers from retrieval failures, context fragmentation, and information loss; long-context LLMs suffer from the "lost in the middle" phenomenon, quadratic attention costs, and hallucinations in needle-in-haystack scenarios.

### EVIDENCE
- `https://arxiv.org/html/2501.01880v1` — "Long Context vs. RAG for LLMs: An Evaluation and Revisits" (Li et al., Dec 2024): LC generally outperforms RAG on QA benchmarks especially Wikipedia-based; summarization-based retrieval performs comparably to LC; RAG better for dialogue/general queries.
- `https://arxiv.org/html/2407.16833` — Google DeepMind / EMNLP 2024 Industry Track (Li et al., 2024): "LC consistently outperforms RAG in terms of average performance" when sufficiently resourced; RAG's cost advantage is significant; proposed Self-Route hybrid reducing cost by 65%.
- `https://aclanthology.org/2024.emnlp-industry.66/` — ACL Anthology page confirming the above paper: Published at EMNLP 2024 Industry Track, pages 881–893.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.09977` — LaRA benchmark (Li et al., Feb 2025, ICML 2025): "No Silver Bullet for LC or RAG Routing" — 2326 test cases, 11 LLMs evaluated; optimal choice depends on model size, long-text capability, context length, task type, and retrieved chunk characteristics.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.04939` — LooGLE benchmark (ACL 2024): 24k+ token documents, 6,000 questions; reveals that long-context LLMs still struggle with long-dependency understanding despite expanded context windows.
- `https://arxiv.org/html/2402.13718v3` — ∞Bench (Zhang et al., ACL 2024): First LLM benchmark with average data length >100K tokens; tasks designed so simple retrieval is insufficient; evaluates long-context capability beyond retrieval-based shortcuts.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05856` — "Seven Failure Points When Engineering a RAG System" (Barnett et al., 2024): 7 documented failure points including chunking issues, retrieval failures, and LLM reliance limitations.
- `https://arxiv.org/html/2401.05856v1` — Full HTML of the above, describing 3 case studies (research, education, biomedical), 15,000 documents, 1,000 QA pairs.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.03172` — "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts" (Liu et al., 2023, TACL): Performance degrades significantly when relevant information appears in the middle of long contexts; documents the U-shaped attention curve.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.11230` — MMNeedle benchmark (NAACL 2025): Even GPT-4o suffers from hallucination when needles are not in haystacks; reveals performance gap between API-based and open-source models.
- `https://cynthialmy.github.io/2026-02-06-rag-insights-limitations/` — "RAG in Practice: Where It Works, Where It Fails" (2026): Documents three failure modes: context noise, fragmentation, cost explosion; identifies event-to-concept gap where embeddings fail.
- `https://www.meilisearch.com/blog/rag-vs-long-context-llms` — Meilisearch blog (Nov 2025): Side-by-side comparison covering grounded responses, retrieval quality trade-offs.
- `https://www.databricks.com/blog/long-context-rag-performance-llms` — Databricks blog: Analysis showing increasing context doesn't always help RAG; benefits of longer context for RAG accrue only when more retrieved documents fit.
- `https://aiagentmemory.org/articles/context-window-problem-llm/` — Documents O(N²) attention scaling, context window limitations, and RAG as the primary mitigation strategy.

### CHANGES
None.

### RISKS
- **Coverage gap**: The "Retrieval meets Long Context" ICLR paper (openreview.net) and the LaRA paper at ICML 2025 were identified but the full LaRA HTML and the ICLR paper were not fully fetched. The metadata/abstracts are sufficient for source inventory but not for deep claims extraction. Mitigation: fetch full PDFs in a follow-up deep-read phase.
- **Access restrictions**: The ACM DL (403) and Medium (403) sources were blocked by Cloudflare. Only arXiv and direct-access sites yielded content. Mitigation: use alternative mirrors or cached versions for blocked sources.
- **Recency bias**: Most papers span 2023-2025; the 2026 landscape may have shifted with models like Gemini 2.5 and GPT-4.1. Already partially mitigated by the Meilisearch (Nov 2025) and RAG limitations (Feb 2026) blog posts.
- **Negative evidence depth**: The negative queries yielded fewer direct empirical comparisons and more practitioner blog posts about failure modes. The "Lost in the Middle" and MMNeedle papers provide the strongest empirical negative evidence for long-context LLM limitations.

### BLOCKERS
None.

agents.prompt:
You are Screener 2 — an independent relevance reviewer for a rapid evidence assessment.

Research Question: "How do RAG and long-context LLMs compare for document question answering tasks?"

## Your task:
Review the following 10 sources and judge each for relevance to the RQ. Consider:
- Does the source directly compare RAG and long-context LLMs?
- Does it provide empirical evidence (benchmarks, metrics, comparisons)?
- Is it from a credible venue (peer-reviewed, benchmark, technical)?

## Sources to screen:

| ID | Title | URL | Type | Year |
|----|-------|-----|------|------|
| W1 | Retrieval Augmented Generation or Long-Context LLMs? A Comprehensive Study and Hybrid Approach | https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.16833 | paper | 2024 |
| W2 | Long Context vs. RAG for LLMs: An Evaluation and Revisits | https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01880 | paper | 2025 |
| W3 | Long Context vs. RAG: Strategies for Processing Long Documents in LLMs | https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3726302.3731690 | paper | 2025 |
| W4 | RAG vs Long-Context LLMs: A Comprehensive Comparison | https://medium.com/@rosgluk/rag-vs-long-context-llms-a-comprehensive-comparison-9b30594c445e | article | 2025 |
| W5 | LongBench v2 | https://longbench2.github.io/ | benchmark | 2025 |
| W6 | InfiniteBench: Extending Long Context Evaluation Beyond 100K Tokens | https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.13718 | paper | 2024 |
| W7 | Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented Generation System | https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05856 | paper | 2024 |
| W8 | The Top 5 RAG Failure Modes | https://insightedge.technology/2025/11/03/the-top-5-rag-failure-modes-and-how-to-fix-them/ | article | 2025 |
| W9 | Why RAG Fails: Every Failure Mode and How to Fix Each One (2026) | https://krunalkanojiya.com/blog/why-rag-fails | article | 2026 |
| W10 | Solving the Lost in the Middle Problem | https://www.getmaxim.ai/articles/solving-the-lost-in-the-middle-problem-advanced-rag-techniques-for-long-context-llms/ | article | 2025 |

## Output format (ONLY this — nothing else):

```
INCLUDE: W1,W2,W3,W4,W5,W6,W7,W8,W9,W10
RELEVANCE: W1=5,W2=5,W3=4,W4=3,W5=4,W6=4,W7=4,W8=3,W9=3,W10=3
```

Where INCLUDE lists the IDs you would include in the synthesis, and RELEVANCE gives each a score 1-5.

Be especially critical: does W7 (RAG failure points) directly compare RAG vs LC? Does W10 (lost in the middle solutions) address the comparison question or just one side?

agents.result:
I now have sufficient evidence from all sources. Here is my assessment:

---

### SUMMARY
I reviewed all 10 sources against the RQ "How do RAG and long-context LLMs compare for document question answering tasks?" Three sources (W1, W2, W3) are direct empirical comparisons between RAG and LC — the core evidence. Four sources (W4, W5, W6, W10) are partially relevant: one is a lower-credibility direct comparison (W4), two are benchmarks for the LC side (W5, W6), and one addresses both paradigms tangentially (W10). Two sources (W8, W9) are exclusively about RAG failures with no LC comparison and are excluded. W7 (RAG failure points) is borderline but included for background context on RAG limitations.

### EVIDENCE
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.16833` (W1): Abstract confirms "comprehensive comparison between RAG and long-context (LC) LLMs" with benchmarks across public datasets using three LLMs. Accepted at EMNLP 2024 industry track. Proposes hybrid Self-Route method.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01880` (W2): Abstract states "revisits recent studies... more comprehensive evaluation... LC generally outperforms RAG in question-answering benchmarks... RAG has advantages in dialogue-based... queries." Direct empirical comparison with datasets.
- `https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3726302.3731690` (W3): Title "Long Context vs. RAG: Strategies for Processing Long Documents in LLMs." ACM venue (peer-reviewed), 2025. Blocked by Cloudflare but title and venue confirm direct comparison.
- `https://medium.com/@rosgluk/rag-vs-long-context-llms-a-comprehensive-comparison-9b30594c445e` (W4): Medium blog, blocked by Cloudflare. Title indicates direct comparison but blog format reduces credibility.
- `https://longbench2.github.io/` (W5): Benchmark page confirms focus on evaluating long-context LLM capabilities (503 MCQs, 8K–2M tokens). No RAG-vs-LC comparison; evaluates LC side only.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.13718` (W6): Abstract states "first LLM benchmark featuring an average data length surpassing 100K tokens... making simply retrieving a limited number of passages from contexts not sufficient" — implicitly contrasts with retrieval but does not empirically compare RAG vs LC.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05856` (W7): Abstract describes an "experience report on the failure points of RAG systems from three case studies." No mention of long-context LLMs or comparison between paradigms.
- `https://insightedge.technology/2025/11/03/the-top-5-rag-failure-modes-and-how-to-fix-them/` (W8): WordPress blog. Title and content about RAG failures only. No LC comparison.
- `https://krunalkanojiya.com/blog/why-rag-fails` (W9): Personal blog (2026). Content about RAG failure modes. No LC comparison.
- `https://www.getmaxim.ai/articles/solving-the-lost-in-the-middle-problem-advanced-rag-techniques-for-long-context-llms/` (W10): Article from Maxim AI company blog. Metadata shows it discusses "long-context LLM, RoPE decay... RAG techniques" — engages both paradigms but as a solutions/how-to piece, not empirical comparison.

### CHANGES
None.

### RISKS
- **W3 confirmed by title/venue only**: Cloudflare blocked access to the ACM abstract page, so the full abstract could not be read. However, the title "Long Context vs. RAG" and the ACM venue (peer-reviewed) provide sufficient confidence for scoring.
- **W8 and W9 may contain passing references to LC**: Only truncated HTML was retrieved for both blogs. There is a small risk they contain relevant comparative content deeper in the article, but based on titles and visible content they appear exclusively RAG-focused.
- **W4 credibility**: Medium blog posts are not peer-reviewed and may contain unverified claims. Included at a lower relevance score (3) as supplementary perspective.

### BLOCKERS
None.

agents.result:
Now I have sufficient evidence from the abstracts and search results. Let me compile my assessment.

---

### SUMMARY
I reviewed all 10 sources by examining their abstracts, venues, and descriptions via Semantic Scholar API, web search snippets, and the LongBench v2 homepage. Sources W1 and W2 are the strongest — both directly compare RAG and long-context LLMs with empirical benchmarks. W3 is a directly on-topic SIGIR tutorial. W4 is on-topic but a Medium blog. W5 and W6 are long-context benchmarks that are relevant context but not themselves RAG-vs-LC comparisons. W7 catalogs RAG failure modes (relevant background). W8 and W9 are pure RAG-failure blog posts with no LC comparison — excluded from synthesis. W10 bridges RAG and long-context via the "lost in the middle" problem.

### EVIDENCE
- `https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/ArXiv:2407.16833?fields=title,abstract,year,venue` — W1 abstract: "We conduct a comprehensive comparison between RAG and long-context (LC) LLMs... We benchmark RAG and LC across various public datasets using three latest LLMs." Venue: EMNLP 2024.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01880` (web search snippet) — W2 abstract: "Extending context windows (i.e., Long Context, LC) and using retrievers... (i.e., Retrieval-Augmented Generation, RAG) are the two main strategies... This paper revisits recent studies... We then provide a more comprehensive evaluation."
- `https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3726302.3731690` (web search snippet) — W3: "This half-day tutorial offers a unified, beginner-friendly introduction to Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Long Context methods... equipping IR researchers and practitioners with actionable guidelines." Venue: SIGIR 2025.
- `https://medium.com/@rosgluk/rag-vs-long-context-llms-a-comprehensive-comparison-9b30594c445e` (web search snippet) — W4: "RAG and long-context LLMs are two approaches to handling complex language tasks in 2025... This comparison evaluates their underlying..." Medium blog.
- `https://longbench2.github.io/` — W5: "LongBench v2 is designed to assess the ability of LLMs to handle long-context problems requiring deep understanding and reasoning across real-world multitasks." No mention of RAG-vs-LC comparison on homepage.
- `https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/ArXiv:2402.13718?fields=title,abstract,year,venue` — W6 abstract: "tasks are designed to require well understanding of long dependencies in contexts, and make simply retrieving a limited number of passages from contexts not sufficient for these tasks." Venue: ACL 2024. Implies RAG limitations but is not itself a RAG-vs-LC comparison.
- `https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05856` (web search snippet) — W7: "we present an experience report on the failure points of RAG systems from three case studies." No LC comparison mentioned.
- `https://insightedge.technology/2025/11/03/the-top-5-rag-failure-modes-and-how-to-fix-them/` (web search snippet) — W8: "Here are the top 5 failure modes and actionable fixes" for RAG systems. No mention of LC comparison.
- `https://krunalkanojiya.com/blog/why-rag-fails` — W9: Title "Why RAG Fails: Every Failure Mode and How to Fix Each One" — personal blog, RAG-only focus.
- Web search on W10 — Title: "Solving the Lost in the Middle Problem: Advanced RAG Techniques for Long-Context LLMs" — blog article bridging RAG and long-context issues.

### CHANGES
None.

### RISKS
- W3, W5, W6 were assessed from abstracts/snippets only (no full-text access). The ACM paper (W3) was blocked by Cloudflare; web search provided its abstract. Full-text access might reveal richer empirical content, potentially raising W3's score to 5.
- W4, W8, W9, W10 are blog articles — their content may have shifted or been paywalled; the assessments rely on search snippets and titles.

### BLOCKERS
None.
